interpretations.description:
The concept of the Book of Thoth was adopted by the Hermetic tradition, which identified
                    Thoth with the Greek Hermes and created the figure of Hermes Trismegistus ("Thrice-Greatest
                    Hermes"). The Hermetic texts, written in the early centuries CE, claimed to preserve
                    the secret teachings of Thoth/Hermes.
                
                
                    In the Western occult tradition, the "Book of Thoth" became associated with various
                    magical texts and grimoires. In the 20th century, Aleister Crowley titled his
                    interpretation of the Tarot "The Book of Thoth," connecting the concept to esoteric
                    card reading. The Egyptian Book of the Dead has also sometimes been confused with
                    or identified as the Book of Thoth.

sources.name: The Book of the Dead
sources.description: A collection of spells and texts intended to guide the deceased through the afterlife. While not explicitly the Book of Thoth, it represents a similar genre of Egyptian funerary literature associated with Thoth's role as scribe of the gods.
sources.title: The Book of the Dead

sources.name: Hermetica
sources.description: A collection of writings attributed to Hermes Trismegistus, a syncretic figure combining the Greek god Hermes with the Egyptian god Thoth. These texts explore philosophical and alchemical themes.
sources.title: Hermetica

sources.name: Greco-Egyptian Magical Papyri
sources.description: A collection of papyri containing spells, rituals, and magical formulas from the Greco-Roman period in Egypt. These papyri often invoke Thoth (Hermes) and refer to secret knowledge and books.
sources.title: Greco-Egyptian Magical Papyri

sources.name: The Demotic Story of Prince Neferkaptah
sources.description: An ancient Egyptian story written in the Demotic script, which tells the tale of Prince Neferkaptah's quest for the Book of Thoth and the consequences that follow.
sources.title: The Demotic Story of Prince Neferkaptah

sources.name: Corpus Hermeticum
sources.description: A collection of 17 hermetic treatises written in Greek and Latin, probably between the 1st and 3rd centuries CE. These texts delve into cosmology, philosophy, and spiritual attainment, often associated with Hermes Trismegistus and the pursuit of hidden knowledge.
sources.title: Corpus Hermeticum

longDescription:
The Divine Grimoire of Egypt's God of Wisdom The Book of Thoth is a legendary Egyptian magical text said to have been written by Thoth
                    himself, the ibis-headed god of wisdom, writing, magic, and the moon. According to
                    ancient sources, this book contained only two spells, but these spells granted their
                    reader godlike powers over the cosmos itself.

extendedContent.content:
The Book of Thoth is a legendary Egyptian magical text said to have been written by Thoth
                    himself, the ibis-headed god of wisdom, writing, magic, and the moon. According to
                    ancient sources, this book contained only two spells, but these spells granted their
                    reader godlike powers over the cosmos itself.
                
                
                    The book was hidden by Thoth within multiple nested boxes at the bottom of the Nile,
                    guarded by serpents and magical protections. Despite its dangers, the allure of its
                    power drew seekers throughout Egyptian history, most famously the prince Setne Khaemwas.
extendedContent.title: Description and Contents

extendedContent.content:
The most famous myth about the Book of Thoth comes from the Demotic tales of Setne
                    Khaemwas, a historical prince and high priest of Ptah who became legendary for his
                    magical knowledge. In the story, Setne learned that the book had already been found
                    by a previous seeker, the prince Naneferkaptah, and lay with him in his tomb.
                
                
                    Naneferkaptah told Setne his tragic tale: how he had braved the Nile's depths, defeated
                    the serpent guardian, and obtained the book. But Thoth's vengeance was swift. Naneferkaptah's
                    wife and son both died, and he himself drowned, the book remaining with his body in death.
                
                
                    Setne took the book anyway, but suffered such supernatural torments that he was forced
                    to return it to the tomb. The tale serves as a warning: divine knowledge is not meant
                    for mortals, and those who seek to steal the gods' secrets will pay a terrible price.
extendedContent.title: The Tale of Setne Khaemwas

extendedContent.content:
The concept of the Book of Thoth was adopted by the Hermetic tradition, which identified
                    Thoth with the Greek Hermes and created the figure of Hermes Trismegistus ("Thrice-Greatest
                    Hermes"). The Hermetic texts, written in the early centuries CE, claimed to preserve
                    the secret teachings of Thoth/Hermes.
                
                
                    In the Western occult tradition, the "Book of Thoth" became associated with various
                    magical texts and grimoires. In the 20th century, Aleister Crowley titled his
                    interpretation of the Tarot "The Book of Thoth," connecting the concept to esoteric
                    card reading. The Egyptian Book of the Dead has also sometimes been confused with
                    or identified as the Book of Thoth.
extendedContent.title: Later Traditions

keyMyths.title: Thoth's Authorship
keyMyths.description: The Book of Thoth is believed to have been written by Thoth himself, the Egyptian god of wisdom, knowledge, and writing. It is said to contain powerful spells and secrets that could grant immense power to the reader.
keyMyths.source: Various Hermetic texts and later Egyptian magical papyri

keyMyths.title: The Cursed Knowledge
keyMyths.description: Many legends state that the knowledge contained within the Book of Thoth is dangerous and can corrupt or destroy those who are not prepared to wield its power. Only those with a pure heart and strong will can safely access its secrets.
keyMyths.source: Later interpretations found in Greco-Roman Egypt and medieval grimoires.

keyMyths.title: The Prince Neferkaptah and the Book
keyMyths.description: One popular story involves Prince Neferkaptah, who sought the Book of Thoth hidden in a chest at the bottom of the Nile. He braved dangerous trials to obtain it, only to bring tragedy upon himself and his family due to the book's power.

keyMyths.title: Immortality and Control over Elements
keyMyths.description: The Book of Thoth is said to grant the reader the ability to understand the language of animals, control the elements, and even achieve immortality. This power is, however, often depicted as coming at a great cost.
keyMyths.source: Greco-Egyptian magical papyri and alchemical texts.

keyMyths.title: The Book's Disappearance
keyMyths.description: The ultimate fate of the Book of Thoth is shrouded in mystery, with various accounts claiming it was hidden away, lost to time, or even fragmented into multiple scrolls or texts. Its continued existence remains a topic of speculation and legend.
keyMyths.source: Various occult traditions and fictional adaptations.
